sections.context: Burrow V1 is the OS-isolated sandbox runtime layer for the os-eco coding-agent ecosystem (greenhouse → overstory → sapling/claude-code). Today, agents either run on the host (polluting toolchains, risky filesystem access) or inside Docker (heavyweight, slow). Burrow wraps native OS sandbox primitives — bwrap on Linux, sandbox-exec on macOS — so any CLI-based coding agent runs in a workspace-scoped jail with declared network policy and explicit env passthrough. V1 is local-first and single-user; the BurrowProvider and AgentRuntime seams are designed so remote substrates (fly/AWS) and new agents land as additions, not rewrites. This epic decomposes the work per SPEC.md §22.
sections.approach: Implement V1 in 11 sequential phases (0-10) per SPEC.md §22, totaling ~3-4k LOC over 12-16 focused days. Each phase produces a working slice end-to-end against its scope so we can verify SPEC.md §23 success criteria incrementally. Architecture is Bun + TypeScript strict, citty CLI on top of a typed Client library, drizzle-on-bun:sqlite for durable state, p-queue for per-burrow concurrency. Native sandbox wrappers (no srt, no Docker) so Burrow owns the contract. Two seams kept first-class: BurrowProvider (LocalProvider in V1, FlyProvider stub) and AgentRuntime (claude-code, sapling, codex built-ins plus declarative AgentConfig). Each phase becomes a child seed that can be sd plan submit'd individually when picked up — this top-level plan deliberately stops at phase granularity to avoid freezing detail before we learn from earlier phases.

alternatives.name: Use Anthropic's srt (sandbox-runtime) wrapper instead of writing native bwrap/sandbox-exec
alternatives.rejected_because: Adds a third-party CLI dependency in the critical path of daily workflow and gives up ownership of the profile contract. Future provider implementations (Fly, AWS) need to generate equivalent profiles directly anyway. Native primitives are stable OS interfaces; ~500-800 LOC that changes rarely once correct (SPEC.md §8.6).

alternatives.name: Docker-based isolation
alternatives.rejected_because: OS-level sandbox primitives are dramatically lighter than containers, with no image management, no daemon, no per-burrow pull. Burrow is explicitly not a container manager (SPEC.md §2.3).

alternatives.name: Build remote provider (Fly) in V1 alongside LocalProvider
alternatives.rejected_because: Doubles scope. The BurrowProvider seam earns its keep regardless; shipping the second implementation can be additive post-V1 (SPEC.md §3.2).

alternatives.name: Postgres + Redis + HTTP server from day one
alternatives.rejected_because: V1 is local-first and single-user — SQLite WAL handles durable state in-process, no daemon, no external services. Reversibility for future server modes is preserved by drizzle's multi-dialect support (SPEC.md §6).

alternatives.name: Big-bang implementation rather than 11 phases
alternatives.rejected_because: Each phase produces a verifiable slice tied to SPEC.md §23 success criteria. Phasing also means quality gates run continuously and we discover spec gaps early.

steps.title: Phase 0 — Scaffold the Bun/TypeScript project skeleton
Phase 1 — Native sandbox wrappers (bwrap + sandbox-exec) with SSH passthrough and network policy
Phase 2 — SQLite data model, drizzle schema, repos, and per-burrow run loop with crash recovery
Phase 3 — Workspace materialization: git worktree for project burrows, fork for task burrows
Phase 4 — AgentRuntime interface + built-ins (claude-code, sapling, codex) + declarative AgentConfig
Phase 5 — Inbox + steering: messages table, send/chat commands, next-turn injection
Phase 6 — Event tail + archive: in-memory pub/sub, logs/events --follow, NDJSON archiver on destroy
Phase 7 — Library API + full CLI surface: Client class, all commands wired, JSON/pretty modes, branding pass
Phase 8 — burrow.toml schema, secrets resolution, toolchain doctor (with op:// 1Password support)
Phase 9 — burrow ship: build + deploy artifacts with fly and render as first ShipTargets
Phase 10 — Polish: README, examples, error message review, branding compliance, shell completions

risks: bwrap and sandbox-exec have subtly different capability models; profile drift between Linux and macOS could let one platform succeed where the other silently leaks. Mitigation: shared SandboxProfile contract validated by integration tests on both platforms.

git worktree against the host clone gives the agent read-write access to .git/, so a misbehaving agent could corrupt host git metadata. Mitigation: read_only_main_branch flag plus path-policy constraints (SPEC.md §11.2).

Spawn-per-turn vs one-shot runtimes diverge enough that the inbox-injection abstraction may leak. Mitigation: encodeInboxMessage hook on the runtime + clear CLI surfacing when targeting a one-shot runtime (SPEC.md §13.3).

Network policy enforcement on Linux: userspace HTTP proxy vs nftables. The latter is more correct but less portable. Decision deferred per open question §25.3 — ship userspace proxy first, nftables opt-in later.

Drizzle is the only abstraction layered on bun:sqlite. If drizzle's bun:sqlite adapter regresses, we may hit blockers. Mitigation: keep schema and queries simple enough that we could drop to raw bun:sqlite if needed.

Toolchain mounting strategy (specific binary vs full $PATH ancestry) interacts badly with shim-based managers (mise, asdf, fnm). Decision deferred per open question §25.4 — V1 mounts resolved binary + lib dir, shim-aware mode via burrow.toml flag.

Claude Code credential cache forwarding is convenience-critical but couples us to Anthropic's auth layout. Mitigation: detect-and-skip-on-failure rather than hard requirement; user can always log in inside burrow.
acceptance: burrow up in a project with a burrow.toml succeeds and agents run inside an isolated sandbox with the host filesystem outside the workspace unreachable (SPEC.md §23).
Four parallel burrows (one project + three task burrows) can run agents simultaneously without races.
kill -9 on the burrow process leaves the system recoverable: in-flight runs marked failed, burrows still listed, workspaces intact.
burrow send <id> "<msg>" is delivered on the next turn for claude-code and sapling runtimes.
burrow events --follow interleaves events from all active burrows in real-time as NDJSON.
burrow destroy <id> archives the burrow's complete event log to ${dataDir}/archive/<id>/events.jsonl.
Adding a Gemini-CLI-shaped agent requires only a [[agents]] entry in burrow.toml — zero code changes in src/.
burrow doctor correctly identifies a missing host toolchain declared in burrow.toml and refuses to up until fixed.
A new IC running `bun install -g @os-eco/burrow-cli && cd project && burrow up` is productive without further setup.

A future FlyProvider can be added without modifying any file under src/core/, src/db/, src/runtime/, src/inbox/, src/events/, or src/runner/ — the seam holds (the load-bearing test of the architecture).
bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck pass cleanly across the whole codebase at the end of every phase.

sections.context: burrow watch is the first post-V1 feature per SPEC §24 (immediately ahead of burrow serve and the future web UI). The naive approach is to write a self-contained TUI that pokes ANSI codes inline against repos/event-bus queries (overstory's dashboard.ts is shaped that way). The opportunity is to build it as the pre-web-UI: a stable DashboardSnapshot view-model is the load-bearing seam, and today it drives a terminal renderer while tomorrow it drives a React frontend over burrow serve. Same shape, two faces. This mirrors the precedent already in the spec — the §14.1 NDJSON event envelope is the exact same pattern for events. Building the snapshot contract now means burrow serve is a thin wrapper later, not a rewrite, and we gain a --json mode immediately that scripts/CI can consume.
sections.approach: Layer the feature so the view-model (data) is decoupled from the renderer (presentation). Three files own the contract: src/dashboard/types.ts (DashboardSnapshot, BurrowCard, RunSummary, EventTailEntry shapes — additive-only versioning convention same as §14.1 events), src/dashboard/snapshot.ts (pure builder over Repos), and src/dashboard/stream.ts (async generator coalescing event-bus pushes + cooperative-abort polling fallback, mirroring the patterns already established in src/events/tail.ts + src/events/poll.ts). Two more files own the TUI face: src/dashboard/tui-state.ts (pure key-handling reducer over a viewState) and src/dashboard/tui-render.ts (pure renderSnapshot(snap, viewState, termSize): string returning the screen as one ANSI blob; golden-tested). The runtime (alt-screen, raw mode, resize, keypress dispatch) lives in src/dashboard/tui.ts and is the only impure piece. The CLI command parses args and chooses TUI vs --json mode; --json emits raw DashboardSnapshot NDJSON (the exact shape burrow serve will WebSocket-stream). Zero runtime deps beyond what burrow already pulls — raw ANSI like overstory but layered. Finally we lock the contract publicly: re-export types from the library entry and add SPEC §27 'Dashboard view model' documenting the envelope, the additive-only rule, and the burrow serve forward-compat plan.

alternatives.name: Adopt Ink (React for terminal)
alternatives.rejected_because: Heavy runtime dep and the goal is to feed a future React web frontend, not run React in the terminal. Raw ANSI keeps the terminal renderer thin and makes the view-model — not the renderer — the load-bearing seam. Conflating view-state and render also makes future web reuse harder, not easier.

alternatives.name: Use blessed / blessed-contrib
alternatives.rejected_because: Same heavy-dep / niche-maintenance reasons as Ink, and they conflate layout with rendering — exactly the seam we want to keep clean.

alternatives.name: Copy overstory's dashboard.ts shape (single file, ANSI inline)
alternatives.rejected_because: Works for a one-shot TUI but bakes the renderer into the data layer. When burrow serve lands, we'd have to re-derive the snapshot shape; until then we'd have no --json mode for scripts/CI. The decoupling cost is one extra file (snapshot.ts) and pays back the moment the web UI starts.

alternatives.name: Build burrow serve + React web UI first, skip the TUI
alternatives.rejected_because: SPEC §24 puts burrow watch ahead of burrow serve in roadmap order, and dogfooding parallel agent work is a TUI-shaped problem today. Designing the snapshot contract while building the TUI is what makes burrow serve cheap later — not the other way around.

alternatives.name: Ship only --json mode, defer the TUI
alternatives.rejected_because: Kills the immediate UX win and the 'first post-V1 feature' mandate. The TUI is also the forcing function that proves the snapshot contract supports a real renderer.

steps.title: Define DashboardSnapshot view-model types (src/dashboard/types.ts) and lock additive-only versioning convention
Implement pure buildSnapshot(repos): DashboardSnapshot builder with synthetic-DB unit tests
Implement streamSnapshots(repos, bus, opts) async generator — event-bus driven with coalescing + abort-aware polling fallback
Implement pure TUI view-state reducer (src/dashboard/tui-state.ts) for keypress → viewState transitions (q/j/k/enter/esc, scroll, focus)
Implement pure renderSnapshot(snap, viewState, termSize): string with golden tests covering layout + resize
Implement TUI runtime (src/dashboard/tui.ts): alt-screen entry/exit, raw-mode keypress dispatch, resize listener, snapshot-stream consumer, clean SIGINT shutdown
Wire burrow watch CLI command (src/cli/commands/watch.ts) — TUI mode plus --json NDJSON snapshot mode mirroring burrow events; register in src/cli/main.ts

Lock public surface: export DashboardSnapshot/buildSnapshot/streamSnapshots from src/index.ts; add SPEC §27 'Dashboard view model' (envelope, additive-only rule, burrow serve forward-compat); update CHANGELOG; bump to 0.2.0

risks: Bun raw-mode TTY input may have rough edges vs Node — verify keypress handling (especially modifier keys, paste, terminal resize signals) on both macOS and Linux during step 6 before locking the API.

Terminal resize race: a redraw mid-resize can produce torn output. Mitigation: debounce SIGWINCH and re-render only on the trailing edge; cover with a deterministic test that simulates resize via injected termSize.

Snapshot rebuild cost at high event volume — N burrows × M events/s could hammer SQLite. Mitigation: coalesce within a configurable window (default 50–100ms) and rebuild from scratch (cheap at V1 scale) rather than diffing.

View-model drift: once burrow serve ships, removing or renaming a field would break web clients. Lock additive-only in SPEC §27 from day one; don't ship anything optional that we're not committed to. Treat the snapshot envelope with the same discipline as §14.1 events.

TTY-detection mismatch with --json: defaulting to TUI when stdout is a TTY but JSON when piped (mirroring burrow events) is the right behavior but easy to break. Add a CLI test that asserts both branches.

Detail-pane scrollback memory: holding all events for a focused burrow in memory can grow unbounded. Cap the in-memory tail at a sensible window (last N=500 events) and document it; the SQLite store remains source of truth for replay.

Snapshot stream resource leak: if the generator returns without closing the bus subscription, listeners pile up. Mitigation: try/finally around the subscription and an integration test that opens/closes 100 streams and asserts bus listener count returns to zero.
acceptance: burrow watch in a workspace with ≥1 active burrow renders a multi-panel TUI that updates live as agents emit events, with header / burrow list / detail pane / keybind footer.

burrow watch --json emits NDJSON DashboardSnapshot envelopes to stdout — the exact shape burrow serve will eventually WebSocket-stream — and is consumable by a scripted reader (assert via piped test).
DashboardSnapshot, BurrowCard, RunSummary types are exported from @os-eco/burrow (src/index.ts) and documented in SPEC §27 with the additive-only versioning rule explicit.
renderSnapshot(snap, viewState, termSize) is pure: golden tests pass for fixed inputs across 80×24, 120×40, and a narrow 60×20 layout.
Keypress reducer handles q (quit), j/k (move selection), enter (focus burrow detail), esc (back to list), PgUp/PgDn (scroll detail tail) — covered by unit tests on simulated key sequences.
Ctrl+C while watching cleanly exits the alt-screen buffer (no leftover ANSI state, cursor restored, terminal usable). Verified by integration test that captures stdout post-exit.
Snapshot stream coalesces a burst of N=100 events within the coalescing window into ≤2 snapshot emissions, and always includes the latest state.
Snapshot stream subscription is leak-free: opening and closing 100 streams against a shared bus leaves zero residual listeners.
burrow events --follow and burrow watch can run against the same database concurrently without one starving the other (verified by parallel integration test).
All quality gates pass: bun test, bun run lint (--error-on-warnings), bun run typecheck. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] → [0.2.0] is added and version bumped via scripts/version-bump.ts.
